name: Vernon Parish
short_description: Explore Vernon Parish for quartz, agate, and jasper specimens in Louisiana's Kisatchie National Forest region. Flat terrain and accessible public lands make this a good destination for beginners an...
description:
Vernon Parish sits within the Kisatchie National Forest area of central Louisiana, a region rich in Quaternary and Tertiary sedimentary deposits. The gentle, flat landscape at approximately 244 feet elevation hosts scattered quartz nodules, agates, and jasper fragments weathered from ancient fluvial and lacustrine deposits that underlie the Louisiana coastal plain.

This area lies at the transition zone between Louisiana's upland pine forests and its lower coastal plain, creating diverse geologic exposures. Weathering and erosion have exposed silica-rich materials throughout washes, exposed banks, and forest floors. Rockhounders here typically find small to medium quartz specimens, banded agates, and colorful jasper pieces that have been naturally liberated from their host sediments over thousands of years.

The accessibility and beginner-friendly terrain make Vernon Parish an excellent starting point for Louisiana rockhounders. Unlike many Western locations requiring extensive hiking or 4x4 access, Vernon Parish allows exploration on foot with minimal elevation gain. The Kisatchie-Mora Road corridor provides access to multiple collecting areas within reasonable driving distance of Leesville.

Expect to find specimens in the 1 to 4 inch range for most quartz and agate material. Patience and careful scanning of disturbed soil and roadside banks yield the best results. This location is ideal for developing field skills in specimen identification and learning to recognize subtle color variations in Louisiana's quartz varieties.
history: Vernon Parish's geology reflects the Pleistocene and Tertiary history of the Louisiana coastal plain, where fluvial systems deposited silica-rich sediments over millions of years. Weathering of these ancient formations has concentrated quartz and agate nodules in the soils and stream washes visible today, making the region a natural repository of silica-based specimens.
tips: Search along roadside banks, especially where fresh erosion has exposed soil layers - these are prime spots for quartz and agate. Use a hand rake or small shovel to gently disturb the upper soil and duff layer in forest areas; specimens often rest just below the surface. Look for translucent or milky-white quartz and banded agate material in warm tan, rust, and cream tones. Early morning or after rain provides the best visibility and softer ground for digging.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 244 feet elevation with minimal slope. Dense pine forest with sandy to clay soils. Walking distances from parking are typically short, under 1 mile. Footing is generally stable but can be soft or muddy after rain. Some areas have dense undergrowth requiring careful navigation.
best_season: March - May, September - November. These periods offer mild temperatures, lower humidity, and less risk of heat exhaustion or seasonal flooding. Summer brings intense heat and high humidity; winter can be wet and cold.
written_directions: From Leesville, take LA 117 north approximately 12 miles toward the Kisatchie National Forest area. Look for pullouts and forest access roads near Kisatchie-Mora Road. Vernon Parish rockhounding sites are scattered throughout the national forest boundary; consult local Forest Service maps for specific collecting areas and access points. GPS coordinates 31.221483, -93.107857 mark a central location within the parish.
road_conditions: LA 117 is paved; secondary roads and forest roads are unpaved and may be washboard or soft after rain. Standard passenger vehicles can access main areas.
parking_notes: Forest Service pullouts and informal parking areas along Kisatchie-Mora Road and forest access roads. No designated lot; expect small informal areas with space for 2-4 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Forest Service regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or equipment. Collect only loose material and surface specimens. Respect private property boundaries and stay on public land.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Collect loose surface material only
Respect private property and stay on public lands
nearest_services: Leesville, approximately 12 miles south. Gas, food, water, and supplies available in town.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain with minimal elevation change makes this accessible to most hikers and families. No wheelchair access to collection areas due to uneven forest floor and lack of developed trails. Sturdy footwear recommended for soft or muddy ground. Walking distances are short, typically under 1 mile from parking.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding in Vernon Parish, Louisiana?
faq.answer: Vernon Parish is known for quartz, agate, jasper, and chert specimens. Most finds are 1 to 4 inches in size, ranging from translucent milky quartz to banded agate in cream, tan, and rust tones. Colorful jasper fragments are also common in this Kisatchie National Forest area.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound in Vernon Parish?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on Kisatchie National Forest lands in Vernon Parish. You must follow the 25 lb per day limit and collect only loose, surface material. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: Is Vernon Parish a good rockhounding destination for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. Vernon Parish offers flat, easy-to-navigate terrain with minimal hiking required. Specimens are accessible on or near the surface, making it ideal for learning to identify quartz and agate. The short walking distances and forgiving landscape suit families and novice rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound in Vernon Parish?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the best conditions. These seasons provide mild temperatures and lower humidity. Summer heat and winter wet conditions make collecting less comfortable and efficient.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding near Leesville?
faq.answer: Bring a hand rake, small shovel, and screen for sifting loose soil. A backpack, water, hat, and sunscreen are essential in Louisiana's climate. Sturdy boots help navigate soft or muddy forest ground. A collection bag and small GPS unit are useful for marking productive areas.

faq.question: How far is Vernon Parish from Leesville, and how do I get there?
faq.answer: Vernon Parish is approximately 12 miles north of Leesville. Take LA 117 north from town and look for pullouts along Kisatchie-Mora Road near the national forest boundary. Forest Service roads provide access to multiple collecting sites within the parish.

meta_description: Find quartz, agate, and jasper in Vernon Parish, Louisiana. Easy public land rockhounding near Leesville on Kisatchie National Forest. Beginner-friendly.
